title: OpenAI Launches GPT-5.5-Cyber With Restricted Access for Cybersecurity Professionals
summary: OpenAI is rolling out GPT-5.5-Cyber exclusively to vetted cyber defenders — even as CEO Sam Altman previously criticized this exact access-gating playbook when Anthropic used it for Mythos.
content: OpenAI is preparing to launch GPT-5.5-Cyber, a specialized version of GPT-5.5 built for cybersecurity work including penetration testing, vulnerability identification, and malware reverse engineering, with access gated to verified 'critical cyber defenders' through an application process. The UK's AI Security Institute evaluated the model on 95 cyber tasks and found GPT-5.5 achieves a 71.4% pass rate on Expert-level tasks — outperforming Anthropic's Mythos Preview (68.6%), GPT-5.4 (52.4%), and Claude Opus 4.7 (48.6%), leading AISI to call it 'the strongest model we have tested.' GPT-5.5 also completed AISI's 'The Last Ones' simulation — a 32-step corporate network attack modeling a full enterprise intrusion kill chain — in 2 of 10 attempts, making it the second model ever to do so after Mythos. The restricted rollout carries notable irony: Sam Altman had previously called this exact access-gating strategy 'fear-based marketing' when Anthropic used it for Mythos, and observers noted that an unauthorized group reportedly still managed to access Mythos. The dual availability of GPT-5.5-Cyber and Anthropic's Claude Security marks a turning point where AI-powered vulnerability scanning moves from experimental to production-grade, but access decisions remain opaque and industry-governed.

title: GPT-5.5 Is OpenAI's Most Capable Agentic AI Model Yet
summary: OpenAI launched GPT-5.5 as its most capable agentic AI model to date, designed to plan, use tools, and complete multi-step tasks independently with major benchmark gains in coding, long-context reasoning, and web browsing.
content: OpenAI launched GPT-5.5 on April 23 as 'a new class of intelligence for real work and powering agents,' calling it the most capable agentic AI model to date — built from the ground up to plan, use tools, check its own output, and work through tasks independently without constant human course-correction. It is the first retrained base model since GPT-4.5, co-designed with NVIDIA's GB200 and GB300 NVL72 rack-scale systems, and scores 82.7% on Terminal-Bench 2.0 (vs. 69.4% for Claude Opus 4.7) and 73.1% on Expert-SWE, a benchmark where tasks carry a median estimated human completion time of 20 hours. In long-context retrieval, GPT-5.5 scores 74.0% on the MRCR v2 benchmark at one million tokens, compared to only 36.6% for GPT-5.4, and GPT-5.5 Pro leads all publicly available models on OpenAI's web-browsing benchmark at 90.1%. API pricing is set at $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens for standard access, with GPT-5.5 Pro priced at $30/$180 per million tokens for deeper parallel reasoning. OpenAI reports that over 85% of its own employees now use Codex weekly with GPT-5.5, and chief scientist Jakub Pachocki noted that 'the last two years of model progress had felt surprisingly slow' — framing this release as a genuine step change.

title: Anthropic Launches Claude Security in Public Beta for Enterprise Customers
summary: Anthropic has opened Claude Security to public beta for Enterprise customers, offering AI-powered code vulnerability scanning and patch generation using Opus 4.7 with no custom tooling or API wiring required.
content: Anthropic has opened Claude Security to public beta for Claude Enterprise customers, bringing AI-powered vulnerability detection directly into production codebases using the Opus 4.7 model — requiring no custom tooling or API integration for organizations already using Claude. The platform scans code for vulnerabilities, validates each finding to reduce false positives, and generates suggested patches that developers review and approve before deployment, aiming for a higher signal-to-noise ratio than traditional static analysis tools. Based on feedback from hundreds of early adopters since a February 2026 research preview, new capabilities include scheduled recurring scans, directory-level targeting, CSV and Markdown exports, real-time webhook notifications to Slack and Jira, and persistent dismissals that carry forward across scans to reduce alert fatigue. Anthropic framed the launch as a direct response to AI-powered cyberattacks accelerating faster than defenders can keep up, noting that models like Mythos have dramatically shortened the window between vulnerability discovery and exploitation. The tool integrates with major security platforms including CrowdStrike, Microsoft Security, Palo Alto Networks, SentinelOne, and Wiz, and is being deployed through consulting partners including Accenture, BCG, Deloitte, Infosys, and PwC.

title: US and Allies Publish Joint Guidance on Securing AI Agent Deployments
summary: CISA, NSA, and cybersecurity agencies from Australia, Canada, New Zealand, and the UK jointly warned that agentic AI is already deployed in critical infrastructure with insufficient safeguards and published concrete security recommendations.
content: Cybersecurity agencies from the US (CISA and NSA), Australia, Canada, New Zealand, and the UK jointly published guidance urging organizations to treat autonomous AI systems as a core cybersecurity concern, warning that agentic AI is already deployed in critical infrastructure and defense sectors without adequate safeguards. The guidance identifies five categories of risk: excessive privilege grants that amplify the blast radius of a compromise; design and configuration flaws that create gaps before systems go live; unpredictable behavioral drift where agents pursue goals in unintended ways; structural cascade failures across interconnected agent networks; and accountability gaps where opaque decision-making makes post-incident analysis difficult. Key recommendations include assigning each agent a cryptographically secured identity with short-lived credentials, encrypting all agent-to-agent communications, and requiring human sign-off for high-impact actions — with the agencies emphasizing that deciding which actions need approval is the responsibility of system designers, not the agent. The document specifically flags prompt injection — where malicious instructions embedded in data can hijack agent behavior — as a critical and potentially unsolvable problem that all organizations deploying agentic systems must plan around. The agencies acknowledged the security field has not caught up with agentic AI, advising organizations to 'prioritise resilience, reversibility and risk containment over efficiency gains' until evaluation standards and best practices mature.

title: Colorado's Third Attempt to Revise Its Landmark AI Law Focuses on Consumer Notification
summary: Colorado lawmakers introduced Senate Bill 189, scaling back the state's first-in-the-nation AI regulation from requiring algorithmic transparency to simply notifying consumers when AI makes consequential decisions about them.
content: Colorado introduced Senate Bill 189, its third attempt to revise the state's first-in-the-nation 2024 AI regulation law, shifting from requiring companies to disclose how their algorithms make decisions on hiring, loans, and housing to simply notifying consumers that AI was used and giving them a right to appeal or request human review. The bill pushes the law's effective date back from June 2026 to January 2027, and Senate Majority Leader Robert Rodriguez — who authored the original law — described it as 'more of a notice bill,' acknowledging it is less comprehensive but calling it a necessary compromise between tech industry pressure and consumer protections. The legislation was introduced just weeks after the DOJ joined a lawsuit filed by Elon Musk's xAI challenging Colorado's existing law as 'unconstitutionally vague' and arguing it would 'disproportionately burden small businesses and start-ups.' Senate Bill 189 also clarifies developer and deployer liability by shielding developers from penalties arising from deployers' misuse of their technology, and includes a three-year grace period on civil penalties under the Colorado Consumer Protection Act before enforcement kicks in. Consumer advocacy groups including the AFL-CIO, AARP Colorado, and the ACLU of Colorado said they are 'cautiously optimistic,' while tech industry representatives called it 'a marked improvement' over the original law — though both sides remain unhappy with specific provisions.

title: HBR Survey: Most Enterprises Have AI in Production but Only 16% Report High Measurable Value
summary: A Harvard Business Review Analytic Services survey of 385 business leaders finds that while 59% of organizations have AI in production, most are gaining only incremental productivity boosts because AI remains a standalone tool rather than being embedded in workflows.
content: New research from Harvard Business Review Analytic Services, conducted in March 2026 with 385 business decision-makers, found that while 59% of organizations have AI in production, only 16% report realizing a high degree of measurable value — with the majority describing returns as moderate (33%) or slight (36%). AI adoption is strongest for productivity improvements (64% see impact) and operational efficiency (58%), but metrics tied to strategic growth — new revenue streams (30%) and ROI (35%) — are the least likely to have improved, pointing to a widening gap between AI deployment and business transformation. Only 18% of respondents say AI is primarily integrated within their workflows; the rest use it as a standalone tool alongside existing processes, which fundamentally limits its ability to drive higher-level outcomes. Among organizations that have successfully embedded AI into workflows, 71% report realizing substantial or moderate value — compared to just 16% overall — making workflow integration the clearest predictor of ROI. Key barriers to scaling AI include legacy systems (cited by 69% as limiting), siloed data (34%), lack of integration across systems (31%), and insufficient AI talent (30%), with 92% of respondents agreeing that AI agents require rules-based guardrails yet fewer than half of organizations having defined them.
